coredata.dc:title: Silicon phthalocyanine-decorated TiO2 mesocrystal coupled with multifunctional all-carbon structure for multistep cascade signal amplifier in photoelectrochemical immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yilin
Zhang, Shupei
Dai, Hong
Lin, Yanyu
Zeng, Baoshan
Hong, Zhensheng
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a new photoelectrochemical sensor for aflatoxin B 1 (AFB1) detection was established based on the all-carbon nanohybrids as transducer scaffold accompanied with quasi-octahedral TiO2 mesocrystal (QOTM) absorbed dentritic Si phthalocyanines (SiPcs) as the efficient bioprobe. Specifically, the all-carbon nanocomposites exhibited fine photoelectric properties due to the photo-generated electrons of carbon quantum dots could be rapidly accepted by conductive carbon nanohorns, which evidently expedited the electron transport, and the nanohybrids were first employed as affinity support to anchor AFB1 antibody. The dentritic SiPcs sensitized QOTM presented better photocurrent response and stability in comparison with QOTM alone due to impressive light-harvesting capability of the dentritic SiPcs. Under light irradiation, the photo-induced electrons of SiPcs migrated to the QOTM, and successively transferred to carbon quantum dots as electron relay mediator then via carbon nanohorns to the electrode surface based on suitable band alignment. This hierarchical charge transport cascade paradigm efficiently impeded the charge recombination, resulting in noticeably increased photocurrent. In this competitive immunosensor, the AFB1 standards compete with the labeled AFB1, leading to photocurrent decreased with the increasing target concentration in a wide linear range of 10−6–102 ng/mL. The newly developed methodology provided a versatile approach for ultrasensitive detection of small molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical biosensor
Carbon quantum dots
TiO2 mesocrystal
Si phthalocyanines
Aflatoxin B 1

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bis-pentyl-carboxylate SiPcs and the preparation of AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs bioconjugates
Preparation of all-CNS materials modified electrode
Construction of the competitive PEC sensor for AFB1
EIS and PEC characterization of the biosensor
The PEC mechanism for the AFB1 detection
Optimization of the PEC biosensing platform and experimental conditions
Analytical performance of the signal-off biosensor toward the target AFB1
Specificity, reproducibility, and stability of the PEC biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Fuzhou Science and Technology Bureau

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation of Fujian Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: New Century Talent Project of Fujian Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fujian Province Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talents

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was financially supported by the NSFC ( 21205016 , 21575024 ), National Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2016J06003 , 2016J05026 ), Education Department of Fujian Province ( JA14071 , JK2016009 , JA13068 ) and Foundation of Fuzhou Science and Technology Bureau ( 2015-S-160 , 2015-G-72 ) and New Century Talent Project of Fujian Province ( WER1059 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM, TEM (inset) and SAED (inset) images of QOTM, (B) UV–visible adsorption and FL (inset) spectra of dentritic SiPcs, (C) TEM image of CQDs, (D) UV–visible adsorption (a) and FL (b) spectra of CQDs.

(A) Applied potential bias-dependent photocurrent densities, (B) IPCE spectra at applied potential of 0V, (C) Voc time profile and (D) electron lifetimes measurements (determined from the Voc decay in dark) of (a) QOTM and (b) QOTM/SiPcs in 0.1M PBS (pH8.0).

(A) EIS spectra and PEC response of various modified electrodes. (a, a’) GCE; (b) GCE/CNHs, (b') GCE/CQDs; (c, c') GCE/CNHs/CQDs; (d, d') GCE/CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1; (e, e') GCE/CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1/AFB1; (f, f') GCE/CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1/AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs. The concentration of AFB1 was 10ng/mL. The EIS spectra were obtained in 5.0mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1mol/L KCl. The PEC tests were performed in 0.1M PBS (pH8.0) under the applied potential of 0V.

(A) Photoactive species characterizations of (a) QOTM, (b) QOTM@SiPcs, (c) CQDs/QOTM@SiPcs, and (d) CNHs/CQDs/QOTM@SiPcs in 0.1M PBS (pH7.0). (B) Photocurrent responses of (a) CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1/AFB1, (b) CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1/AFB1-QOTM, (c) CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1/AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs in 0.1M PBS (pH8.0). The concentrations of anti-AFB1 and AFB1 were 3μg/mL and 10ng/mL, respectively. The inserts in Fig. 4B was the corresponding histogram comparison chart. All experiments were performed at 0V applied potential.

(A) The effect of different concentrations (a–h) 10−6–102 ng/mL AFB1 on differential photocurrent density. (B) The corresponding calibration curve. (C) Selectivity of the immunoassay toward AFB1 (0.1ng/mL) determined by comparing with interfering agents (10ng/mL) and corresponding mixture. (D) Time-based photocurrent density measured with light switched on and off toward 10−6 ng/mL AFB1. The experiments were recorded at 0V applied potential in 0.1M PBS (pH8.0).
(A) Fabrication procedure of the PEC immunosensor and (B) the mechanism of the photo-initiated electron transfer pathways and the structure of dentritic SiPcs.

head.ce:title: Silicon phthalocyanine-decorated TiO2 mesocrystal coupled with multifunctional all-carbon structure for multistep cascade signal amplifier in photoelectrochemical immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Quantum Manipulation and New Energy Materials, College of Physics and Energy, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Quantum Manipulation and New Energy Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a new photoelectrochemical sensor for aflatoxin B 1 (AFB1) detection was established based on the all-carbon nanohybrids as transducer scaffold accompanied with quasi-octahedral TiO2 mesocrystal (QOTM) absorbed dentritic Si phthalocyanines (SiPcs) as the efficient bioprobe. Specifically, the all-carbon nanocomposites exhibited fine photoelectric properties due to the photo-generated electrons of carbon quantum dots could be rapidly accepted by conductive carbon nanohorns, which evidently expedited the electron transport, and the nanohybrids were first employed as affinity support to anchor AFB1 antibody. The dentritic SiPcs sensitized QOTM presented better photocurrent response and stability in comparison with QOTM alone due to impressive light-harvesting capability of the dentritic SiPcs. Under light irradiation, the photo-induced electrons of SiPcs migrated to the QOTM, and successively transferred to carbon quantum dots as electron relay mediator then via carbon nanohorns to the electrode surface based on suitable band alignment. This hierarchical charge transport cascade paradigm efficiently impeded the charge recombination, resulting in noticeably increased photocurrent. In this competitive immunosensor, the AFB1 standards compete with the labeled AFB1, leading to photocurrent decreased with the increasing target concentration in a wide linear range of 10−6–102 ng/mL. The newly developed methodology provided a versatile approach for ultrasensitive detection of small molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins, toxic secondary metabolites generated from fungal species, present in extensive range of foodstuff and feed product [1], which have detrimental effects, such as mutagenicity, immune suppression and hepatocellular carcinoma, on humans' health [2]. Aflatoxin B 1 (AFB1) is the most frequently found mycotoxin and highly toxic one among the aflatoxins [3], which is ranked as group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer [4]. To monitor the contamination levels of AFB1, a host of researches have been performed, such as electrochemical assay [5], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6], UHPLC-MS/MS [7], electrochemiluminescent (ECL) [8] and förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [9] etc. Nevertheless, it is still urgent and critical to develop validated sensing techniques for rapid, accurate, and cost effective detection of AFB1 at low concentration levels. The newly emerged photoelectrochemical (PEC) protocol with tremendous potential could be regarded as the next-generation detection platform owing to its high sensitivity, short response time, and simple apparatus [10]. This promising analytical methodology that based on the connection between the change of photocurrent and target concentration has become a powerful passport in the fields of food security, environmental monitoring, and clinical diagnostics [11]. Typically, the method commonly consists of sandwich-type and competitive-type PEC assay formats. Although the high specificity and sensitivity of sandwich-type detection platform, there exist several drawbacks, such as the lower photo-absorption rate, longer incubation time and more chemicals consumption due to the utilization of the two matched antibodies [6]. Besides, for the small molecules examining, the competitive-type matrix could offer a more appreciative pattern, since target analyte would be monitored by virtue of its capability to compete with the labeled antigen/hapten [6,12]. Conventionally, the detectable response mainly stems from the labeled enzyme that executes the oxidation or reduction reaction toward substrate to construct the probe components. Nevertheless, there exist some disadvantages of enzyme labeling, such as the unsatisfactory reproducibility, high cost of sensor fabrication, and instability for long-term experiment or clinical practice. One promising approach is to synthesis an effective tag that substitute enzyme to bypass these drawbacks and expanding the PEC immunoassays [13]. Up to present, the prevalent used photoactive materials are predominantly based on n-type inorganic semiconductor nanospecies [14,15] or quantum dots [16–18], and the TiO2 has turned out to be the prime candidate due to its multitudinous superiority, for example, the fine biocompatibility, eminent photoelectric activity, and environmental safety [19]. To further elevate the substantial light harvesting ability and hinder photo-excited electron-hole (e−-h+) charge carriers recombination of TiO2 (large band gap: ∼3.2eV), heterojunction construction and coupling with narrow-band semiconductor were commonly utilized synthetic strategies. Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are considered as efficient photo-sensitizer to harvest light on surface of the semiconductor and have become a research hotspot in frontier areas, for instance, dye-sensitized solar cells [20], electrochromic and electroluminescent displays [21], non-linear optics [22], photovoltaic and semiconductors [23] etc. The metalloid silicon (IV) Pcs (SiPcs, a p-type semiconductor) are a particularly attractive subclass of Pcs given the low toxicity levels of silicon and their low band gap (~1.7eV) [24]. Additionally, SiPcs play an important role in developing solar light photocatalysts owing to their excellent electronic and optical properties, especially the unique absorption in red and near infrared regions and strong electron-donating character [25]. However, the hydrophobic nature of Pcs rings lead to strong tendency to aggregation in solution and limiting their applications [26]. The more efficient way to suppress the aggregation of SiPcs is to attach dentritic substituents to the axial position of silicon centre [27]. This dendrimer SiPcs not only prevent self-aggregation, but also enhance the electron transfer efficiency. Herein, a facile strategy has been developed to intimately integrate quasi-octahedral TiO2 mesocrystal (QOTM) with dentritic SiPcs as the signal amplification label (QOTM@SiPcs). The mesoporous QOTM could directly composite with the filling guest material of the dentritic SiPcs due to the enlarged contact area. By combining a p-type material with an n-type semiconductor, the resulting p/n heterostructure perform efficient visible-light absorption and charge separation, presenting an eminent probe tag for signal amplification.

Apart from the exquisite probe design in PEC sensing platform, the basal transducer scaffold construction is also critical for bioprocessing applications and obtaining favourable photocurrent response. In this paper, a new-type all-carbon nanohybrids sensitization (all-CNS) electrode system was developed based on the synergistic interaction between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanohorns (CNHs) to facilitate heterogeneous electron-transfer processes. Recently, fluorescent CQDs have been attracted countless attention, however, the investigations on PEC properties and applications are still rare. Compared with two-dimensional zero bandgap graphene, the 0-dimensional CQDs possess an intrinsic bandgap for photochemical reaction because of their higher optical activities associated with quantum confinement, edge effects [28] and visible light response [29,30]. Therefore, CQDs-based sensor would be an innovative and meritorious report on photoelectric properties contrary to the traditional fluorescence (FL) sensing approaches. To further improve the photocurrent response, the CNHs with high conductivity could receive the photo-induced electrons from the conduction band (CB) of CQDs to accelerate the electrons transport. The present work revealed that the integration of all-CNS materials provided a favourable model architecture for constructing PEC biosensing scaffold.

In this protocol, based on the specific recognition between antibody and antigen, an ultrasensitive PEC immunoassay for toxin monitoring was designed and the fabrication process was presented in Scheme 1A. Initially, the clean electrode was successively modified with CNHs, carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) and CQDs to form the all-CNS materials modified electrode. Then the COOH of CMC was activated for following AFB1 antibody (anti-AFB1) anchoring. Subsequently, the electrode was immersed into the mixed solution containing AFB1 and QOTM@SiPcs labeled AFB1 (AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs) conjugates to facilitate the competitive immune reaction. Therefore, a “signal-off” PEC platform was fabricated. With the increasing concentration of AFB1, the amount of AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs decreased and the photocurrent response reducing, thus indicating the AFB1 content. The high sensitivity of this immunoassay mainly resulted from the multistep cascade electron transfer. Elaborately, QOTM@SiPcs conjugates could dramatically enhance the photocurrent derived from the increased light absorption of SiPcs as well as the effective electrons transfer from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of SiPcs to the CB of QOTM. Subsequently, the electrons could further arrive at the CB of CQDs due to the effective matching of energy levels between CQDs and QOTM, and then transferred immediately to CNHs, which resulted in enhanced charge separation and an obviously improved photocurrent response. The designed PEC competition immunoassay presented ultrahigh sensitivity, good reproducibility, stability, and specificity, which was an efficient scheme for other small molecule determination.

AFB1, monoclonal anti-AFB1 were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and CMC were purchased from Aipoo-Huamei Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and Maya bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutaraldehyde (GLD) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Sinopham Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP) was acquired from Acros Organics Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was obtained by mixing stock solution of 0.1M NaH2PO4 and 0.1M Na2HPO4 to adjust the pH. Other reagents were of analytical regent grade. CNHs and CQDs (10mg/mL) were prepared with DMF for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bis-pentyl-carboxylate SiPcs and the preparation of AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs bioconjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of bis-3-methyl-butoxy SiPcs was described briefly below. Initially, SiPcCl2 (0.20g, 0.33mM) with anhydrous K2CO3 (0.14g, 1.01mM) and hexanoic acid (0.5mL, 3.99mM) in dried toluene (30mL) was refluxed at 110°C for 48h. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature and the solvent was evaporated to dryness under reduced pressure. The solid product was purified for 24h by soxhlet extraction using dichloromethane (DCM) as an extracting agent. The extracted material was recrystallized, washed with methanol and deionized (DI) water (v:v=1:1), and then re-dissolved in DCM. The AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs bio-conjugates were prepared as followings. Initially, the synthetic dentritic SiPcs (30μL) was added in 10μL of 10mg/L QOTM for 1h to allow the sufficient adsorption, and the unconjugated SiPcs were removed by centrifugation and washed with DI water. Thereafter, 20μL of 0.1mmol/L 4-ATP was dropped onto the mixed solution containing QOTM@SiPcs conjugates and incubated for 4h. Following that, the unbounded 4-ATP was removed by centrifugation and washed with DI water. Finally, 40μL of 10ng/mL AFB1 was added in the resulting solution for 3h, and then centrifuged and washed by DI water. With the aid of 4-ATP, QOTM@SiPcs could be labeled onto the AFB1 due to the formation of a strong bond (TiS) between the thoil end of 4-ATP molecule and QOTM, and the remaining amino group could form an imine bond with AFB1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of all-CNS materials modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 3μL of 3mgmL−1 CNHs were coated on the clean surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE; 3mm in diameter), and then placed under an IR lamp until the solvents were evaporated and cooled to room temperature. Afterwards, 3μL CMC (0.5wt%) was thrown on the CNHs modified electrode for 40min to allow the reaction between carboxyl group (COOH) at conical terminals of CNHs [31] and hydroxyl (OH) of CMC. And then the electrode was washed with ultrapure water and dried at room temperature to avoid the exfoliation of CNHs. Sequentially, CQDs were deposited on the electrode, dried in the IR lamp and cooled down to room temperature, and the all-CNS materials modified electrode was fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the competitive PEC sensor for AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: Concisely, anti-AFB1 (5μL, 3.0μM) was immobilized on the all-CNS materials modified electrode surface using EDC/NHS as cross-linking. In short, 10μL of EDC (10mM) and NHS (20mM) aqueous solution was dropped onto the GCE/CNHs/CQDs electrode surface to active the COOH group of CMC, and kept in dark for 1h at 4°C, followed by anchoring anti-AFB1. After being dried, the obtained electrode was coated with BSA (5μL, 1.0wt%) at 4°C for 1h. Finally, the electrode was immersed into the mixed solution containing AFB1 standard solution and QOTM@SiPcs labeled AFB1 to facilitate the competition. The photocurrent was measured in pH8.0 PBS at the applied potential of 0V. After each step, the sensor was washed with DI water.

The fresh peanut was purchased from a local market of agriculture products and ground finely with a laboratory mill. Subsequently, 6g of sample was placed in a 50mL centrifuge tube, and 18mL of methanol/water (80:20, v/v) containing 4% NaCl was added [32] to facilitate the sample to be extracted via vortex mixing. After centrifugation at 4000rpm for 5min, 1.2mL of supernatant was transferred to a 3.0mL tube and diluted with 1.8mL of pure water. The resulting solution was used as blank sample for this analytical method.

To get insight into the morphologies and structures of the QOTM, typical scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were provided. As shown in Fig. 1A, the size of crystallites with rough surface was 25–50nm, and the octahedral was built by monodisperse nanoparticles subunits to construct the porosity structure. By using QOTM with large surface area, more dentritic SiPcs molecules could fill into the inner portion of the mesoporous QOTM, forming a close contact structure with TiO2 mesocrystal. To further investigate the organization of anatase TiO2 nanostructure, TEM (inset in Fig. 1A) was performed. It could be observed that the quasi-octahedral shape was constructed from very tiny nanoparticles, which was corresponding to the results of SEM image. Herein, the unique-ordered TiO2 quasi-octahedral was introduced as photo-sensitizer element in this PEC determination.

The UV/vis spectra of dendrimer SiPcs with terminal groups in DMF are shown in Fig. 2B, which presented typical spectra for Pcs with a B band at 355nm, a dimer band at 604nm, and a Q band at 678nm. Additionally, a UV band ranging from 251 to 292nm could be attributed to the absorption of dendritic terminal groups substitutes. Compared with Q band of non-dendrimer SiPcCl2 at 670nm [26], the Q bands of SiPcs with dendritic substitutes were red-shifted 8nm. This outcome demonstrated that the substituent group linked with Si atom via ether bonds could form expanded π conjugation system, thus diminishing the electronic density of Pcs ring and the energy level gap between LUMO and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The inset of Fig. 2B showed the position and intensity of fluorescence (FL) spectra of bis-pentyl-carboxylate SiPcs. The maximum FL emission peak was observed at 694nm, which was in agreement with the perception that electron-withdrawing terminal groups enhanced the rigidity of molecular, reducing the molecular internal conversion.

The topography of CQDs was monitored using TEM (Fig. 1C). The particles exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution and spherical symmetry, with an average diameter of 2–3nm. Additionally, the optical properties of CQDs were tested, which was indicated in Fig. 1D. The UV–vis spectra of aqueous exhibited a broad absorption from 300 to 600nm. Such a high absorbance was conducive for generation of photocurrent under light irradiation. The FL spectra exhibited the emission at ∼529nm when excited at 368nm, originating from the excited electrons returning to ground state and recombined with holes.

Pcs and its derivatives are promising sensitized materials for photochemical energy conversion and storage owing to their excellent light-harvesting properties in the red/near-IR (Q band) regions [24]. Therefore, in this work, Pcs were utilized as sensitizer for wide-bandgap oxide semiconductor. In order to conquer the low efficiency derived from its aggregation and lack of directionality in the excited state, an intriguing symmetrical SiPcs sensitizer containing bis-pentyl-carboxylate were acted as “push” group was designed. The purpose of the bis-pentyl-carboxylate was to minimize the aggregation, enhance solubility, and tune the LUMO level of SiPcs that offered directionality in the excited state. Therefore, the dentritic SiPcs was introduced here to combine with QOTM for acquiring excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency, and a series of PEC measurements were performed to prove this viewpoint. Initially, as shown in Fig. 2A, QOTM@SiPcs presented a larger photocurrent density in contrast with QOTM under the applied potentials from 0.0–0.6V vs Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 5mV/s with chopped light irradiation, indicating that QOTM@SiPcs owned a better PEC performance and the dentritic SiPcs could enhance the light absorption and trigger the charge-carrier separation.

The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) plots were the other fundamental means to illustrate the photoelectric behaviours of the photoactive materials by measuring IPCE values at different wavelengths. The outcome presented in Fig. 2B was according to Eq. (1) [33] with no bias voltage.
(1)
IPCE % =
1240 × j
λ × P
× 100

Where P, λ and j were the incident light power in W·cm−2, wavelength in nm, and short circuit photocurrent density in A·cm−2. Evidently, an elevated IPCE value was observed in whole spectral range for QOTM@SiPcs by contrast to the pristine QOTM, which could be brought to the heterojunction construction that caused wide light absorption and quick electrons transfer. Additionally, it was intriguing to note that the maximum IPCE value associated with the corresponding wavelength for QOTM@SiPcs had a phenomenon of red-shift, further expanding the utilization of optical scope.

Subsequently, Fig. 2C of the typical open-circuit photovoltage (Voc) response was employed from another perspective to elucidate the prominent character of QOTM@SiPcs. Under open circuit condition, the inspired photoelectrons transferred and accumulated within the nanostructure films upon light illumination, causing a more negative potentials shift of the Fermi level and an increase in open-circuit voltage [34]. Accompanied with the competition between charge recombination and photoelectron accumulation, the Voc achieved to a stable state (maximum value) under light irradiation. Once light was stopping, the Voc decayed as the collected electrons were relaxed owing to the recombination with photo-generated holes and the scavenging by electron acceptor species in electrolyte [35]. The significant drop in Voc and slowing decay observed with QOTM@SiPcs indicated that more photo-induced electrons in QOTM@SiPcs were generated and survived longer.

According to this model, the decay lifetime of accumulated electrons could be related to the reduce in potential using Eq. (2) [36].
(2)
τ =
k B
T
e
d
V OC
dt
− 1

Where τ, e, and kBT were the potential dependent lifetime, charge of a single electron, and the thermal energy in which kB was Boltzmann's constant and T was temperature in K. The dVoc/dt was the derivative of open-circuit voltage transient. The relationship between electrons lifetime and Voc in QOTM and QOTM@SiPcs was presented in Fig. 2D. Obviously, QOTM@SiPcs exhibited a longer decay lifetime of the accumulated electrons. These comparison experiments sufficiently demonstrated the admirable photoelectric performance of dentritic SiPcs sensitized QOTM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS and PEC characterization of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was an available way to characterize interface properties and construction procedures of the electrodes. The electron transfer resistance (Ret) was equal to the semicircle diameter at low frequency, which represented the charge transfer resistance of redox probe accessing the layer [11]. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the electrode of GCE/CNHs had a smaller semicircle (curve b) in comparison with bare GCE (curve a), indicating the prominent conductivity for CNHs. After the CQDs were assembled onto the GCE/CNHs electrode, the Ret increased (curve c), implying the formation of new film hindered the access of redox probe to the electrode surface. After gradual immobilization of anti-AFB1 (curve d), the Ret was enlarged, owing to the protein layer midified PEC sensing matrix hindered electron transfer of the electrochemical probe to the electrode surface. The semicircle diameter was further increased when AFB1 anchored on the electrode (curve e), suggesting the successful assembling of AFB1. However, the electrode of GCE/CNHs/CQDs/anti-AFB1 incubated with AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs possessed higher electron-transfer resistance, proving the weak conductivity of QOTM@SiPcs and the conjugates was successfully fabricated.

Except for the investigation of the construction procedures, the photocurrent response of different modified electrodes was explored to certify the feasibility of this biosensor. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the photo-responses increased gradually along with the CQDs (curve b) and CNHs/CQDs (curve c) respectively modified electrode, which revealing that the CQDs owned moderate photocurrent response and the CNHs could efficiently improve the photoelectric performance for CQDs due to their pronounced conductivity. Therefore, this all-CNS photoactive species could be applied as the PEC matrix for basal signal source as well as the scaffold for following immune reaction. The photo-responses declined after immobilization of anti-AFB1 and AFB1, which was attributed to the formation of hydrophobic multilayers that hampered interfacial mass and electron transfer. Nevertheless, the AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs modified electrode showed remarkable enhancement of photocurrent, indicating the successful combination of the tag with the corresponding labeling AFB1. Meanwhile, this phenomenon manifested that the heterostructures could finely promote the photoresponse and this signal amplification strategy afforded a general format for future development of PEC assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PEC mechanism for the AFB1 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Constructing an efficient and delicate bioprobe has a substantial influence on whether the signal to be measured would be sensitive and accurate while the amounts of target are changed. Herein, an advantageous enzyme-free tag comprised of QOTM@SiPcs conjunction was designed and utilized for labeling AFB1 antigen. In comparison with the photoelectric response of pristine QOTM (carve a in Fig. 4A), dentritic SiPcs sensitized QOTM owned an excellent photoresponse (carve b in Fig. 4A) that was two times higher than QOTM. To further certify the feasibility of SiPcs sensitization when applied in the AFB1 monitoring, the other comparison experiment was performed to elucidate the applicability of dentritic SiPcs. As displayed in Fig. 4B, both QOTM (curve b) and QOTM@SiPcs (curve c) labeled AFB1 enlarged photocurrent response compared with the untagged AFB1 (curve a). Nevertheless, from the histogram inserted in Fig. 4B, a remarkable contrast could be observed that the amplification deviation for QOTM@SiPcs (blue) was larger than QOTM (pink), which confirmed that dentritic SiPcs was a viable option in this biosensor.

Additionally, the PEC matrix plays a vital part in enhancing the basal response to avoid the immune reaction process that causes the detection signal to vanish or fade away. Therefore, a fancy all-CNS structure was employed as the photoelectrode scaffold, in which the CNHs substrate with high conductivity could accelerate the electrons transfer and enhance the photocurrent response of CQDs (a series of PEC measurements were performed to verify the sensitization of CNHs for CQDs, please see Fig. S1 in supporting information). Based on the eminent construction of bioprobe and PEC matrix design, a versatile competitive-type immunoassay was established, which connect these two basilica parts through the immune traction. The photo-initiated electrons transfer process of the sensor was shown in Scheme 1B. Under irradiation, photo-generated electrons from the LUMO level of SiPcs could be migrated to QOTM. For the all-CNS structure, CQDs could be acted as a bridging medium to reinforce the flow of electrons that receiving them from the CB of QOTM, therefore increasing the density of photo-generated electrons (carve c in Fig. 4A). Finally, the electrons transferred to the external circuit with the assistance of conductive CNHs, simultaneously improved the photocurrent response (carve d in Fig. 4A). Dentritic SiPcs, QOTM, CQDs, and CNHs presented a multistep cascade band-edge level, which promoted ultrafast transfer of e−-h+ pairs and inhibited charge recombination. In short, this methodology provided an available work for practical use in quantitative small molecular assay for life and food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the PEC biosensing platform and experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better analytical performance, the condition of the biosensing platform construction including the amounts of CNHs, CQDs, absorption time of SiPcs on QOTM, and the ratio between QOTM and AFB1 for construction of the bioprobe were measured. The optimal usage quantity was 3mg/mL for both CQDs and CNHs (Fig. S2A and S2B). As for SiPcs, the absorption time was 1h, which receiving optimal photoelectric response (Fig. S2C). Certainly, the conjugated amounts of QOTM@SiPcs and AFB1 also affect the sensitivity of PEC sensor, and an optimal signal could be acquired at a 2:5 volume ratio of 0.5mg/mL QOTM and 100ng/mL AFB1 (the total volume was 100μL). Additionally, several experimental parameters such as anti-AFB1 and AFB1 incubation time, pH value, and applied potential were also investigated (Fig. S3). Incubation time for anti-AFB1 and AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs were both 1h and the detection measurements were performed in PBS of pH8.0 and applied potential of 0V. The detailed explanation was submitted in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the signal-off biosensor toward the target AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the detect-ability of the PEC bioassay, AFB1 standards with different concentrations were tested on the basis of all-CNS structure accompanied with an efficient signal amplification tag of QOTM@SiPcs for altering the signal change under the optimal conditions. The assay was carried out by using amperometric I-t curves in pH8.0 PBS. As displayed in Fig. 5A, the degree of signal reduction was directly related to the increasing AFB1 concentration. A linear correlation (shown in Fig. 5B) between the photocurrent density and the logarithm of AFB1 concentration was obtained in the dynamic range of 10−6–102 ng/mL with a detection limit (LOD) of 3.2×10−7 ng/mL. Appreciatively, the detection range was wider and the LOD was lower in comparison with other reported previously (Table S1). The regression equation could be fitted to j (μAcm−2)=−0.17464 log C (ng/mL)+1.73972 with R2 =0.9959. Additionally, the feasibility and practicability of this strategy was investigated on real sample of peanuts with recovery rate of 96.8–103.1% (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility, and stability of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Specificity was critical for most of the bioassays, because nonspecific binding would mislead the detection outcomes. The selectivity of the fabricated PEC biosensor was estimated by incubating some interfering antigens including zearalenone (Zea), fumonisins (Fum), streptomycin (Str), and geomycin (Geo) with an excess hundred folds concentration of AFB1 as well as the mixtures of these interferences with AFB1. As shown in Fig. 5C, the detection signal of these interferences was lower than AFB1 and all mixtures had no apparent influence on AFB1 detection, demonstrating the present multiple amplifying biosensor possessed fine specificity.

The reproducibility of the proposed bioassay was assessed by relative standard deviations (RSDs) of both intra-assay and inter-assay. Calculated from the detection results with twelve parallel tests and the RSDs of intra-assay were 2.8%, 3.2%, and 3.6% toward 0.01, 1.0, and 100ng/mL of target AFB1, respectively. The inter-assay RSDs of 4.6%, 5.2%, and 3.8% were obtained by testing the identical samples with twelve biosensors prepared separately at the same experimental condition. These results revealed a commendable reproducibility and satisfactory precision.

The stability of the biosensor was investigated and no obvious change of photocurrent response could be observed within 450s under interrupted light irradiations in Fig. 5D, indicating a good stability of the PEC assay. In addition, the biosensing electrode of GCE/CNH/CQD/anti-AFB1/AFB1-QOTM@SiPcs was stored in a dark and humid environment at 4°C for 2weeks, the photocurrent intensity still retained 94.5% of its starting value, revealing its good long-term storage stability.

In summary, a competitive-type PEC immunoassay for ultrasensitive detection of toxic AFB1 was established based on the cooperation effect of all-CNS structure and PEC bioprobe of QOTM@SiPcs conjugates. As PEC scaffold, the all-CNS structure could noticeably enhance photocurrent owing to admirable properties of absorption of illuminating light, ultrafast transfer of electrons, and efficient suppression of charge recombination. Bioconjugates of AFB1 and QOTM@SiPcs was introduced by specific immune reaction, and the QOTM@SiPcs conjugates could dramatically enhance photocurrent response due to high absorption of exciting light of this heterojunction. More importantly, because of the effective matching of energy levels between SiPcs, QOTM, and CQDs as well as the fast electron migration of the CNHs, the sensing platform showed amplified photocurrent intensity. This protocol not only could be extended to analyse a diverse range of small molecules by using corresponding affinity ligand, but also offered new horizons for providing a reliable and intuitionistic guidance for the development of bioanalytical methods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the NSFC (21205016, 21575024), National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2016J06003, 2016J05026), Education Department of Fujian Province (JA14071, JK2016009, JA13068) and Foundation of Fuzhou Science and Technology Bureau (2015-S-160, 2015-G-72) and New Century Talent Project of Fujian Province (WER1059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.11.014.
